title: e2e: GetRandomIPAddress(): needs to be parallel-safe
body:
The current code is not parallel-safe: https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/32d96f40c3ab46a4c918a9099266748a54cb0772/test/e2e/common_test.go#L489-L490

Sample [failure](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/task/6049777226350592/html/int-podman-rawhide-root-host-sqlite.log.html#t--Podman-checkpoint-podman-checkpoint-and-restore-container-with-file-locks--1):
```
[It] podman checkpoint and restore container with --file-locks
...
# podman [options] run [...] --ip 10.88.180.132 [...]
Error: IPAM error: requested ip address 10.88.180.132 is already allocated to container ID c973ff80056a613f766187c7706f42eaea655b3e58b4abe06f3111db00c70210
```
Simple fix, this is just a placeholder until I (or anyone) gets the round tuit.

comments.body: Duh. I did not check thoroughly enough; this is invoked multiple times within the same test. https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/2a947c2f4b79420d03139d73a2fef9b58411b9f0/test/e2e/checkpoint_test.go#L24-L28

@Luap99 yesterday, in my first attempt at fixing this, [you commented](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/18858#discussion_r1226984765):
>Both CNI and netavark use a sequential ip assignment, which means the first container after boot gets 10.88.0.2 and so on. Therefore I think it would best to force the third octet to 255. That gives us like 60k container starts before we run into the conflict possibility.

How certain are you of this allocation? Is it possible that CNI keeps track of recently-used subnets and starts assigning based on `podman run --ip xxx` subnets?

Reason for asking: I tried a [proof of concept](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/17831/commits/7f6f1a2844c51ef638df5d6cad9d93f6a38d98fd) in which `s/GetRandomIP/GetSafeIP/` and each ginkgo process just gets a sequential `.255.<processid>` incrementing by `ginkgoProcesses`. And what I'm seeing with CNI is that all of a sudden every container without explicit `--ip` gets a `10.88.255.x` address. See [f37 log](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/task/5580285761290240/html/int-podman-fedora-37-root-host-sqlite.log.html#t--Podman-checkpoint-podman-checkpoint-container-with-export--migration---1) and search in-page for `10.88.255`.

Stepping away from this now because I'm completely out of ideas.

> How certain are you of this allocation? Is it possible that CNI keeps track of recently-used subnets and starts assigning based on podman run --ip xxx subnets?

Oh good, you are right it in fact does that based on local testing. They are in fact writing every allocated ip to their last_reserved_ip file and then the next allocation tries to use this ip +1.
Netavark seems to be fine though.
Thank you for confirming. That's going to make test-IP allocation kind of a challenge.

Yes in theory every test that wants a static ip should do `podman network create` and use their own subnet for that. However there is one big problem with that, it is no longer testing `podman run --ip ...` and instead would be testing `podman run --network <name> --ip ...`. I am not sure about how many tests we are talking about but I assume we can convert most of them as long as we can keep one or two who actually test --ip without --network. That should at least lower chances for flakes. And then we can mark the last one or two tests Serial to eliminate flakes completely.
